blocks.text: The **Internet of Things (IoT)** connects billions of everyday physical objects to the network so they can sense, act, and exchange data. This power comes with a hard problem: many of these devices are cheap, tiny, long-lived, and physically exposed, which makes them very difficult to secure. This lesson builds the topic from zero, following the professor's terminology and the ITU-T standards used in the slides.

The current Internet connects countless devices through unified communication frameworks to deliver services directly to users. Experts predict that within a few years **tens of billions** of devices will be connected.

The key idea: IoT bridges the **physical world** with computing power, digital media, data analytics, applications, and services. It benefits many sectors: healthcare, home automation, energy, agriculture, transportation, environment, inventory, security, and education.
A **global infrastructure** that connects physical and virtual *things* via interoperable communication technologies to enable advanced services.

- A **Thing** is any identifiable physical or virtual object linked to communication networks.
- An **IoT Device** is a thing that **must support communication**, and *may* also include sensing, actuation, data capture, storage, and processing.

blocks.title: Communication is the non-negotiable part
blocks.text: An IoT device **may** lack sensors or actuators, but it **cannot** lack communication. Without the ability to communicate, a device simply cannot join a network and is not an IoT device. The other parts (sensing, processing, storage) are optional.

**The new dimension of connection.** Traditional ICT gave us communication *any time* and *from any location*. IoT adds a third axis: communication among **any thing**. That "any thing" axis is what makes the attack surface explode.

Picture a single smart object. Its typical building blocks are:

- a **microcontroller** (a small computer) that provides the "intelligence"
- **sensors** (read physical parameters: temperature, motion) and/or **actuators** (change the physical world: open a valve, switch a relay)
- **network connectivity**, usually via a **transceiver** (radio that sends and receives)
- an **identity / ID** so it can be uniquely addressed

Most IoT devices have *some* computing power, even if very basic, plus one or more of the other parts.

question.prompt: According to ITU-T Y.2060, which capability must every IoT device support?
question.explanation: Y.2060 states an IoT device *must* support communication; sensing, actuation, storage, and processing are optional capabilities it *may* have. A device that cannot communicate cannot join the network, so communication is the defining requirement.

blocks.text: The ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union, Telecommunication Standardization Sector) organizes IoT into **four layers**, with **management** and **security** functions running *across all layers* (vertical, cutting through every level).

steps.label: Device layer
steps.text: The physical devices and how they connect. In OSI terms this maps mainly to the **physical and data-link layers**, handling fundamental connectivity.

steps.label: Network layer
steps.text: Transport and networking that moves data between devices and the core.

steps.label: Service support & application support layer
steps.text: Generic and specific support capabilities for applications.

steps.label: Application layer
steps.text: The actual IoT applications and services.

blocks.title: Two cross-cutting pillars
blocks.text: **Management capabilities** and **security capabilities** are not a single layer at the bottom; they span all four layers. The **security capability layer** provides *generic* security features that stay **independent from the applications**, so the same protections can serve many different services.

At the network's core sit the **application platforms, data repositories, and the network/security management systems**. They collect sensor data, dispatch commands to actuators, and oversee the devices.

At the **edge** sit the IoT devices themselves, ranging from simple resource-limited units to advanced capable ones. **Gateways** sit between them and often perform **protocol translation** and provide extra networking services.

blocks.title: Don't assume edge devices are protected
blocks.text: Systems near the core (and gateways) typically support strong security protocols like **TLS** and **IPsec**. But **resource-limited devices usually have minimal or NO built-in security**, and even sophisticated devices *may* include security features though this is **not guaranteed**. The gateway is therefore crucial: it secures the link between itself and the central platforms. Treating the whole IoT chain as uniformly secure is a classic exam mistake.

A simplified version of the **World Forum IoT Reference Model** describes the framework in layers you should be able to name:

- **Smart Objects / embedded systems** (the edge devices)
- **Edge & Fog Network** (local connectivity)
- **Core Network** (long-haul transport)
- **Data Center / Cloud** (apps, storage, management)

steps.label: Smart Objects / Embedded
steps.text: Sensors, actuators, embedded devices at the edge. Often operate **without physical security**. Need continuous availability, authenticity and integrity of sensor data, protection against unauthorized access and eavesdropping.

steps.label: Edge & Fog Networks
steps.text: Wired/wireless connectivity linking devices. Main challenge: a **diverse mix** of network technologies and protocols, so enforcing one consistent security framework is hard.

steps.label: Core Network
steps.text: Carries data between central hubs and devices. Faces classic core-network risks, but the **huge number of endpoints** amplifies the complexity.

steps.label: Data Center / Cloud
steps.text: Hosts applications, data, and management. IoT adds **no fundamentally new** threats here, except the challenge of managing an enormous number of individual endpoints.

blocks.text: Recommendation **Y.2066** lists the essential security requirements for IoT. Learn these as a checklist:

- **Communication Security** - secure, trustworthy, privacy-respecting communication; prevent unauthorized access; guarantee integrity in transit.
- **Data Management Security** - protect data *storage and processing*; keep data accurate and private at rest.
- **Service Provision Security** - deliver services securely and reliably; prevent fraud and misuse; protect user privacy.
- **Integration of Security Policies and Techniques** - combine diverse policies/methods into consistent protection across heterogeneous devices.
- **Mutual Authentication and Authorization** - device/user **and** platform each verify the other's identity and permissions *before* access.
- **Security Audit** - every access (or attempt) is transparent, traceable, reproducible, and logged across transmission, storage, processing, and use.

blocks.title: Memory hook: "both sides verify"
blocks.text: **Mutual** authentication means it is two-way. The device proves itself to the platform *and* the platform proves itself to the device. A rogue server that only checks the device (but is never checked itself) breaks the requirement.

blocks.title: 6. Why IoT is so hard to secure

blocks.text: The slides describe IoT as one of the **least mature** areas of network security. Beyond cost and sheer scale, several structural challenges make it worse:

- **Unpredictable behavior** - massive numbers and diverse tech produce hard-to-anticipate real-world interactions.
- **Device homogeneity** - many devices share designs and components, so **one vulnerability affects many** (monoculture risk).
- **Deployment challenges** - devices placed in remote/exposed spots are hard to protect physically.
- **Extended life & end-of-support** - devices outlive vendor support and become **"orphaned"** with no patches.
- **Lack of upgrade** - many cannot be updated easily, so users ignore patches.
- **Limited transparency** - users cannot see internal processes or stop unwanted data collection.
- **Absence of alerts** - breaches go unnoticed for long periods.

blocks.title: The orphaned smart camera
blocks.text: A home buys a smart security camera. It works reliably for 8 years. After year 3 the manufacturer **stops releasing firmware** (end of support). A new vulnerability is discovered in year 4. Because the camera is **homogeneous** (millions of identical units), attackers automate the exploit; because there are **no alerts** and the user never checks, the camera is silently recruited into a botnet for years. This single scenario combines four of the listed challenges: long life, end-of-support, homogeneity, and absence of alerts.

The slides group cybersecurity threats in IoT into **three critical areas**:

- **Device failures** - automation controls safety- and property-critical systems; a fault in an IoT heating system could freeze and burst pipes in an empty house.
- **Data breaches** - data is IoT's greatest asset *and* its biggest vulnerability (targeted for marketing abuse, identity theft, stalking, etc.).
- **Cyber attacks** - a compromised device (a smart oven, a tampered sprinkler) can open the whole network. Defenses focus on **securing the weakest link**, continuous monitoring, and controlled access.

question.prompt: A vendor sells 5 million identical smart thermostats. One firmware bug lets an attacker take remote control. Which IoT security challenge does this BEST illustrate?
question.explanation: Device homogeneity means many devices share the same design and components, so a single vulnerability scales to all of them at once. Limited transparency is about users not seeing internal behavior; absence of alerts is about breaches going unnoticed; service provision security is a Y.2066 requirement, not a challenge category.

blocks.text: The slides list the essential security technologies in use for IoT. We will define the most important ones, contrasting the ones students confuse.

Cryptographic methods **designed for resource-limited devices** (RFID tags, sensors, contactless smart cards, medical equipment). They adapt the traditional algorithms used in IPsec and TLS to fit small devices. They still give robust security, but **do not always achieve the ideal balance** between security strength and operational efficiency.

blocks.title: Passwords are a poor fit for IoT
blocks.text: Passwords fail for IoT for two reasons: (1) constrained devices struggle to **store/process** them, and (2) passwords need **human input**, which breaks automated **machine-to-machine** authentication. So password-based protection alone is increasingly unviable for IoT - prefer certificates (PKI), keys in hardware, and MFA.

blocks.term: TEE vs. Secure Element vs. HSM vs. Crypto-processor
blocks.text:
- **TEE (Trusted Execution Environment)** - a **hardware security extension of the CPU** that creates an isolated, shielded area to run critical code and sensitive data, isolated at hardware level from other software (e.g. ARM, Intel, AMD, Qualcomm). It reuses the main processor.
- **Secure Element** - a separate **tamper-proof hardware module** with smart-card-grade security; acts as the device **root of trust**, stores secrets, manages the device lifecycle, and supports over-the-air credential/patch updates.
- **HSM (Hardware Security Module)** - a dedicated physical device (plug-in card or external unit) that generates, stores, and manages keys and offloads asymmetric/symmetric crypto from servers.
- **Secure crypto-processor** - a chip exclusively for crypto, in a tamper-resistant package, ensuring decrypted data/instructions **never leave** the protected boundary; it is the foundation of a security subsystem.

blocks.title: TEE vs Secure Element in one line
blocks.text: A **TEE** carves a protected zone *inside the main CPU* (software isolation enforced by hardware). A **Secure Element** is a *separate dedicated chip*. TEE = part of the processor; Secure Element = its own tamper-proof module acting as root of trust.

- **MFA / 2FA** - require **two distinct forms** of identification, unlike single-factor (SFA) which uses just a password; sharply raises the bar for attackers.
- **PKI certificates** - the roles, policies, and procedures to generate, manage, distribute, store, and **revoke digital certificates** and oversee public-key crypto; used where simple passwords are inadequate and strong identity proof + data integrity are needed.
- **Biometrics** - match a person's unique physical/behavioral traits against a stored template (kept in a secure database or on a protected device like a smart card).

- **Tamper resistance & detection** - vital at device and fog layers (and core) because components sit **outside the secured perimeter**; the device must resist and detect physical tampering.
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)** - assign permissions to **roles**, not individual users; users are then mapped to roles (permanently or temporarily) by duty. Widely used in cloud/enterprise to govern IoT access.
- **Data erasure (wiping)** - overwrite storage with binary data so original data is unrecoverable; should support standards and confirm complete overwrite.
- **Blockchain for IoT** - a decentralized, append-only ledger (transactions grouped into time-stamped, tamper-evident blocks; every node holds a full copy). Integrating it with IoT improves data confidentiality/integrity and lets devices **detect and counter tampering**, building trust between parties.

The slides also note simple but powerful strategies that tie it together: **built-in security** (security in hardware and firmware from the start), **encryption** (at the manufacturing stage and in the field), **risk analysis** (when designing or choosing systems), and **access control** (strict privilege levels and authentication).

question.prompt: An IoT chip designer wants secrets and crypto code to run in an isolated region of the device's MAIN application processor, protected by hardware from the normal OS, without adding a separate dedicated chip. Which technology fits best?
question.explanation: A TEE is a hardware security *extension of the CPU* that isolates critical code and data inside the main processor - exactly the requirement. A Secure Element and an HSM are *separate* dedicated hardware modules, not part of the main CPU. Data erasure is about sanitizing storage, not isolated execution.

blocks.title: The big picture
blocks.text: IoT security is hard because devices are **constrained, homogeneous, long-lived, physically exposed, and rarely updated**. The standards give structure: **Y.2060** defines what IoT is, the **ITU-T 4-layer model** organizes the architecture with security as a cross-cutting pillar, and **Y.2066** lists the security requirements. The countermeasures - lightweight crypto, TEE/Secure Element/HSM, mutual authentication via PKI, MFA, RBAC, tamper resistance, lifecycle management, and blockchain - are the concrete tools that satisfy those requirements on weak devices.

objectives: Define IoT, a Thing, and an IoT device using ITU-T Y.2060, and state the one mandatory capability
Describe the ITU-T four-layer IoT reference model and the World Forum framework layers, including security as a cross-cutting pillar
List the ITU-T Y.2066 security requirements, including mutual authentication and security audit
Explain why constrained IoT devices are hard to secure (homogeneity, end-of-support, lack of upgrades, no alerts, physical exposure)
Identify the main IoT threat areas: device failures, data breaches, and cyber attacks
Select appropriate countermeasures and distinguish TEE, Secure Element, HSM, PKI, MFA, RBAC, tamper resistance, and blockchain
summary: A from-zero lesson on IoT security following the ITU-T standards from the slides: it defines IoT devices and their constraints, walks the four-layer reference model and security landscape, lists the Y.2066 security requirements, surveys the structural challenges and threats, and contrasts the key countermeasures (lightweight crypto, TEE vs Secure Element vs HSM, PKI, MFA, RBAC, tamper resistance, lifecycle, blockchain).
title: Module G: IoT security
